mnemonics.title: Capacity Planning Quick Estimate
mnemonics.content: CUPIDS: Calculate User load, Peak factors, Identify resources, Determine formula, Scale with safety margin

mnemonics.title: Remember Little's Law
mnemonics.content: L Lambda W - Larry (L) looks (λ) wonderful (W)

mnemonics.title: Utilization Threshold
mnemonics.content: 70-80: Don't let your system go to the max, or it'll crash and you'll incur costs

acronyms.usage: Initial capacity estimates within factor of 2-4
Storage performance metric used in quick calculations
Target response times used in capacity planning
Storage provisioning unit for capacity calculations
Storage estimation unit for database capacity planning

visualTips.visualization: Draw an exponential curve: flat below 70%, steep rise after 80%. X-axis: Utilization %, Y-axis: Response Time. Show 80% as 'cliff edge'.
Triangle with L at top, λ and W at base corners. Show: L = λ × W by drawing arrows from λ and W multiplying into L.
Bar chart showing: 1 core = 100%, 2 cores = 160-180%, 4 cores = 240-280%. Show diminishing returns with decreasing incremental gains.
Pie chart: 20% slice labeled 'Critical Resources' (80% load), 80% slice labeled 'Other Resources' (20% load).
Stacked bar: Calculated Capacity (70%) + Buffer (30%) = Total Provisioned. Show buffer as 'safety net' at top.

analogies.analogy: Like estimating travel time by car: 'about an hour' vs GPS precise calculation. Good for initial planning, not for contracts.
Highway at 70-80% capacity flows smoothly; at 100% becomes a parking lot. Same with servers - queues form at high utilization.
Hotel booking 80% capacity leaves rooms for late check-ins; 100% booking means turning away customers.
Adding more lanes to a highway: 1 lane = 100 cars/hr, 2 lanes ≠ 200 cars/hr (maybe 170) due to merging/intersections.
Building bridges 2-3x stronger than needed - capacity planning uses same principle for unexpected loads.
